coredata.dc:title: Improved electrocatalytic performance from graphene quantum dots/three-dimensional graphene/polyaniline doped powder to layer-by-layer self-assembled membrane materials
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Ruibin
Jiang, Yangyang
Jia, Qianqian
Pei, Hebing
Mo, Zunli
Liu, Nijuan
Niu, Xiaohui
Liu, Zhenyu
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, three-dimensional network graphene and graphene quantum dots were prepared using graphite powder as the initial raw material. After that, the method of self-assembly of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the surface of three-dimensional graphene (3D-G) was used to prepare GQDs/3D-G composite to solve the problem of easy agglomeration of graphene in the process of composite preparation, and then it was mixed with polyaniline (PANI) aqueous solution in different contents to form graphene quantum dots/three-dimensional graphene/polyaniline (GQDs/3D-G/PANI) doped composite. The obtained GQDs/3D-G/PANI doped materials exhibited a specific morphology of nanoscale polyaniline firmly attached to the 3D networked graphene layer, which was attributed to the good dispersion of 3D network graphene and polyaniline in water. Graphene quantum dots facilitated close contact between the three-dimensional graphene sheet and the polyaniline particles, which significantly reduced agglomeration. In addition, driven by the strong electrostatic force between the positively charged polyaniline and the negatively charged GQDs/3D-G and the π-π interaction, the nano-scale GQDs/3D-G/PANI thin film layer was obtained by layer-by-layer self-assembly on the hydroxylated ITO plate, which showed the morphology of an interconnected layer network and exhibited good electrochemical catalytic activity for H2O2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Three-dimensional network graphene
Graphene quantum dots
Layer-by-layer self-assembly
π-π Interaction
Electrochemical catalytic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs/3D-G super dispersion system
Preparation of conductive polyaniline aqueous phase dispersion
LBL self-assembly of GQD/3D-G/PANI on ITO substrate
Characterization of functional groups of materials
Characterization of lattice structure of materials
Characterization of thermal stability of materials
Characterization of UV absorption of materials and analysis of influencing factors
Characterization of micromorphology of LBL films
Characterization of surface elements and valence bonds of LBL films
Characterization of the internal structure of LBL films
Study on the electrocatalytic performance of the LBL films

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of GQDs/3D-G/PANI layer-by-layer self-assembled films.
(A) Infrared spectrum of pure PANI, GQDs/3D-G/PANI with different GQDs/3D-G content. (B) XRD spectrum of pure 3DG, GQDs/3D-G/PANI with different GQDs/3D-G content.
TGA curve of PANI and GQDs/3D-G/PANI doped materials with different composition ratios.
SEM images of PAN I(A), GQDs/3D-G (B), GQDs/3D-G/PANI with GQDs/3D-G of 10 wt % (C) and 30 wt % (D).

(A) UV–vis spectra of PANI, GQDs/3D-G and GQDs/3D-G/PANI. (B) UV–vis spectrum variation of PANI solution by addition of different volumetric GQDs/3D-G solution (a-k:0, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 75, 100, 150and 200 μL). (C) UV–vis spectrum variation of PANI solution by addition of different volumetric aqueous acidic solution (a-f：0,10,20,50,100 and 200 μL).

(A) The wide-range XPS spectrum of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10 film. (B) the C 1s XPS data of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10 film. (C) the N 1s XPS data of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10 film. (D) the O 1s XPS data of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10 film.
TEM images of (A) GQDs/3D-G and (B) (GQD/3D-G/ PANI)10.

(A) Cyclic voltammetry curves of bare ITO and (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)0.5 in 10 ml PBS solution (pH = 7.4). (B) Cyclic voltammetry curves of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n/ITO (n = 1, 5, 10, 15) in 10 mL PBS solution(pH = 7.4) and with (C) addition of H2O2 (C = 104 mol L−1).

(A) Cyclic voltammetry curve of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10/ITO in 10 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4) containing H2O2 (C = 5 × 104 moL dm−3), scanning rate:10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100 and 150 mV s-1. (B) The linear relationship of peak current of H2O2 to the square root of scanning rate (A: oxidative peak current and B: reduction peak current).

head.ce:title: Improved electrocatalytic performance from graphene quantum dots/three-dimensional graphene/polyaniline doped powder to layer-by-layer self-assembled membrane materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials
Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, and Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials
Ministry of Education of China, and Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, three-dimensional network graphene and graphene quantum dots were prepared using graphite powder as the initial raw material. After that, the method of self-assembly of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the surface of three-dimensional graphene (3D-G) was used to prepare GQDs/3D-G composite to solve the problem of easy agglomeration of graphene in the process of composite preparation, and then it was mixed with polyaniline (PANI) aqueous solution in different contents to form graphene quantum dots/three-dimensional graphene/polyaniline (GQDs/3D-G/PANI) doped composite. The obtained GQDs/3D-G/PANI doped materials exhibited a specific morphology of nanoscale polyaniline firmly attached to the 3D networked graphene layer, which was attributed to the good dispersion of 3D network graphene and polyaniline in water. Graphene quantum dots facilitated close contact between the three-dimensional graphene sheet and the polyaniline particles, which significantly reduced agglomeration. In addition, driven by the strong electrostatic force between the positively charged polyaniline and the negatively charged GQDs/3D-G and the π-π interaction, the nano-scale GQDs/3D-G/PANI thin film layer was obtained by layer-by-layer self-assembly on the hydroxylated ITO plate, which showed the morphology of an interconnected layer network and exhibited good electrochemical catalytic activity for H2O2.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of modern industry and technology, the primary challenge facing human development is the problem of resource shortage [1]. Fossil fuels stored underground on the global scale are consumed in large quantities, facing depletion, and the climate and environmental problems caused by the misuse of fossil fuels are becoming increasingly apparent [2,3]. Adverse factors such as greenhouse effect and acid rain have begun to affect the global climate. At the same time, with the growth of the global economy and the improvement of human living standards [4], people's consumption and demand for energy are also increasing [5], which put forward new requirements and challenges for the development of new, green and renewable energy sources [6,7].

As one of the conductive polymers with a wide range of applications, polyaniline (PANI) has been used in composites with nano-scale carbon materials to achieve the purpose of improving the electrochemical or catalytic properties of materials [8–11]. As for their synthesis, it is usually achieved by in situ chemical or electrochemical polymerization of aniline/nanocarbon materials. Based on these methods, polyaniline composited with carbon nanotubes (CNTs) or reduced graphene oxide (rGO) shows enhanced specific capacitance and high cycling stability [12,13]. In addition, rGO/PANI composites prepared by in situ chemical oxidation or electrochemical polymerization has significant electrochemical catalytic activity for H2O2, which can serve as non-enzymatic biosensors [14–16]. Even so, the properties of nanocarbon materials/polyaniline are considered to be significantly limited by the aggregation of various components, because the uncontrolled high-speed polymerization of aniline can induce significant chain growth before reaching the surface of nano-scale carbon materials during in situ chemical polymerization or electrochemical polymerization [17]. On the other hand, the aggregation caused by poor dispersion of nano-scale carbon materials and polyaniline in an aqueous system makes the nano-scale carbon material/polyaniline interfacial interaction too weak, thereby reducing the electrochemical performance of the material [18,19]. Therefore, maintaining the monodisperse state of the nano-carbon material and the polyaniline molecule during the compounding process is a key factor for reducing the aggregation of the components, which can further improve the electrochemical performance of the obtained composites [20].

In this paper, we have developed a simple method to further improve the dispersibility of 3D network structure graphene (3D-G) with the aid of in situ self-assembly of graphene quantum dots and solve the problem of easy agglomeration in the reaction process [21], realizing the layer-by-layer self-assembly with polyaniline to prepare electrochemical catalytic materials based on this method [22,23].

In the experiment, GQDs/3D-G/PANI composites were first prepared by doping different contents of GQDs/3D-G in the aqueous dispersion of PANI. Since the monodispersion of 3D-G and PANI by GQDs improved the interfacial interaction, the obtained GQDs/3D-G/PANI composites showed a specific morphology of nano-sized PANI attached to the 3D-G layer. Then the strong electrostatic interaction between electropositive PANI and electronegative GQDs/3D-G and the π-π interaction between the benzene ring structures as driving forces constructed layer-by-layer self-assembly (LBLSA, abbreviated as LBL) GQDs/3D-G/PANI thin film material which showed excellent electrochemical catalytic capacity for H2O2 on the surface of the ITO.(Scheme 1 )

N,N-dimethylacetamide (AR), aniline (AR) were purchased from Shanghai Zhongqin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, 28 %) was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Hydrogen chloride (HCl, 37 %), nitric acid (HNO3, 68 %), (NH4)2S2O8 (AR) were purchased from Yantai Shuangshuang Chemical Co., Ltd. KMnO4 (AR), H3PO4 (AR) and acetone (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) and ethanol (AR) were purchased from Baiyin Chemical Reagent Factory. ITO (<7 ohm/sq, T > 77 %) was purchased from Zhuhai Kaivo Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd. Dialysis bags with a cut-off molecular weight of 3000 Da were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Deionized water was applied throughout all solutions.

Firstly expandable graphite was obtained by two-step intercalation reaction of graphite. Firstly the expandable graphite was transferred to the crucible and then placed in a muffle furnace and heated at 900 °C for 30 s to obtain worm-like expanded graphite. Subsequently, the expanded graphite was ultrasonicated in DMF solution for 24 h, and after filtering, washing and drying, the liquid phase ultrasonic peeled graphene sheet were obtained.

Afterwards 1 g of graphene flake was added to a mixture of acid (90 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid and 30 mL of concentrated phosphoric acid) at 0−5 °C while maintaining an ice bath temperature. After slowly adding 9 g of KMnO4 in continuous stirring, the mixture was heated to 50 °C for a sustained stirring reaction for 12 h.

After the above reaction, 200 mL of ice water and 5 mL of 30 % H2O2 solution were added to the mixed solution at room temperature, and the color of the solution changed to golden. The mixture was centrifuged, washed and dried to obtain graphene oxide. 0.1 g of graphene oxide was dissolved in 100 mL of water, and 1.5 mL of hydrazine hydrate was added after adding ammonia to the solution until the solution was alkaline. The mixed solution was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. After the end of the reaction, the product can be filtered, washed and dried to obtain 3D network-like graphene.

0.1 g 3D-G was dispersed in 120 mL concentrated sulfuric acid and magnetically stirred at 5 °C for 10 min. After slowly adding 0.8 g KMnO4 to the solution, it was heated to 150 °C and stirred at constant temperature for 24 h. The reaction solution was slowly poured into a fast stirred 5 °C ice water bath and then added 3 mL H2O2 to obtain bright yellow GQDs solution. After removing the condensing device, the liquid was completely evaporated by continuous stirring at 85 °C to obtain brown GQDs solids. The resulting GQDs were dispersed in ultrapure water to obtain the GQDs powder after filtrating, long-term dialysis and freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs/3D-G super dispersion system
ce:section.ce:para: Add 15 mg of 3D-G and 15 mg of GQDs to 15 mL diluted HCl aqueous solution (pH = 3.0) and stirred until homogeneous. The pH of the dispersion was adjusted to 6 with ammonia water, and then the mixed solution was subjected to sonication for 30 min to form a dispersion of GQDs/3D-G. The liquid has significant stability and dispersibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of conductive polyaniline aqueous phase dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: The polyaniline was synthesized by adding 15.0 g ammonium persulfate (NH4)2S2O8 into 60 mL HCl solution (1.0 M) containing 6.0 g decompression distilled aniline monomer [24]. The mixture was stirred at 0 °C for 24 h, then the product was filtered and washed with dilute HCl solution, ethanol and deionized water in turn. The resulting product was soaked in ammonia water (1.0 M) for 10 h and finally dried in vacuum at 50 °C. The 200 mg dried dark green polyaniline was added into 10 mL N, N-dimethylacetamide and stirred for 2 h to obtain a uniformly dispersed dark blue solution. Then 40 mL dilute HCl solution (pH = 3.0) was added to form stable aqueous dispersed conductive polyaniline solution.

25 mL of PANI dispersion was mixed with 5 mL of GQDs/3D-G solution, and after stirring for 4 h at room temperature, the mixed system was filtered. The solid was washed with dilute HCl solution, ethanol and deionized water, respectively. After the product was dried in a vacuum at 50 °C, GQDs/3D-G/PANI doped material was synthesized. At this time, the weight content of GQDs/3D-G in the material was 10 wt%. Similarly, doped composites of 20 wt% and 30 wt% were prepared by the above methods.

First, ITO was cut into 2 cm was thoroughly washed by ultrasound in ethanol, acetone and deionized water, and then dried with nitrogen. To produce evenly distributed hydroxyl groups on the ITO surface, the ITO plate was placed in a solution containing 10 mL H2O2, 10 mL NH4OH and 50 mL H2O, oxidized at 80 °C for 30 min. And then it was sonicated in the mixture of H2O/HCl/H2O2 (6:1, v/v/v) for 30 min. Finally, the ITO plate was cleaned with distilled water and moved to an oven at 100 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LBL self-assembly of GQD/3D-G/PANI on ITO substrate
ce:section.ce:para: A piece of hydroxyl functionalized ITO was immersed in the PANI dispersion for 2 h, then washed with deionized water, and dried at room temperature to obtain a layer of polyaniline film on the ITO surface, expressed as (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)0.5 [25]. To induce the assembly of the GQDs/3D-G layer on the ITO surface, the ITO plate was re-immersed into the GQDs/3D-G dispersion for 2 h to form a layer of GQDs/3D-G/PANI on the ITO, which can be expressed as (GQDs/3D-GPANI)1. According to the similar process, the (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n thin film materials were prepared by self-assembly layers on ITO (the specific process was shown in Fig. 1 ).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by FTS-3000 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer in the wavelength range of 400−4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of GQDs/3D-G/PANI powder was performed on a D8 ADVANCE X-ray powder diffractometer. The morphology of GQDs/3D-G/PANI was observed using Ultra Plus type thermal field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) in Zeiss, Germany. And Tencai G-type transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was observed in FEI, USA. The interaction of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n and the LBL process were detected using UV–vis spectra on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. The surface elements of the GQDs/3D-G/PANI composite were tested on a Versaprobe I-type X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS).

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) of the LBL self-assembled (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n membrane was performed in the three-electrode system on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E), where (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n/ITO plate was used as the working electrode, platinum wire was used as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl/KCl electrode was used as the reference electrode, and the phosphate buffer solution (PBS) solution (pH = 7.4) was used as the electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of functional groups of materials
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectrum of the relevant samples was shown in Fig. 1A, which shows that the infrared spectral range of PANI and GQDs/3D-G/PANI is 990−2050 cm−1. In the FTIR spectra of PANI, the absorption peak generated by CN stretching vibration in the quinone ring structure at 1589 cm-1 [26], the absorption peak generated by CC stretching vibration in the benzene ring structure is at 1495 cm-1 [27], and the characteristic absorption peak generated by CN stretching vibration in the benzene ring appears at 1303.67 cm−1 and 1245.81 cm−1, respectively. For GQDs/3D-G/PANI composites doped with 10 wt%, 20 wt% and 30 wt% of GQDs/3D-G, the characteristic peak of PANI at 1589 cm−1 shifted to 1586 cm−1, 1583 cm−1 and 1574 cm−1, indicating that the π-π interaction intensity inside GQDs/3D-G/PANI could be enhanced by increasing the content of GQDs/3D-G [28]. In addition, with the electrostatic interaction between the carboxyl group on the surface of GQDs and nitrogen atoms on the PANI main chain, the induced deformation of the bonds involved causes the FTIR band move to a lower frequency. In addition, in 1495 cm−1, benzene ring CC stretching characteristic peaks of PANI has a similar move [29]. The characteristic peaks of GQDs/3D-G/PANI composites with different GQDs/3D-G doping amounts are 1497 cm−1, 1493 cm−1 and 1487 cm−1. The peak of the material at 1630 cm−1 is caused by the graphite CC stretching vibration [30]. The peaks at 1390 cm−1, 1165 cm−1, and 1058 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibrations of O—H, C—OH, and C—O in GQDs [31], respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of lattice structure of materials
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1B shows the XRD patterns of GQDs/3D-G/PANI composites with different doping amounts. For PANI, two broad diffraction peaks were found at 2θ angles of 19.6° and 23.5°, which are attributed to the diffraction from the (100), (110), and (200) planes [32]. It is known that the higher intensity of the diffraction peak, the stronger sample ordering and the higher crystallinity. Therefore, the intensity of diffraction peak depends on the crystallinity of pure PANI. This is caused by the doping of protonic acid into PANI. Since protonation generates cations, part of the N atoms on the PANI molecular chain and the cations coming in the solution form a "quaternary ammonium salt"-like structure, which enhances the interaction between the molecular chains, so the crystallinity of PANI increases. The XRD patterns for GQD/3D-G/PANI composites are similar to the combination of 3D-G and PANI diffraction patterns. By further comparing the peak width and peak height of various diffraction peaks by the peak fitting function, it can be found that the peak width and peak height of the diffraction peaks of pure polyaniline at 19.6°and 23.5°are similar to those in the composites, which indicates that PANI does not produce crystal structure changes during mixing. At the same time, the wide diffraction peaks at about 20° and 29.7° in the XRD pattern of GQDs/3D-G correspond to the (002) crystal plane of graphite [33], indicating that the oxygen-containing functional groups on the 3D-G surface were removed after the reduction of GO. The XRD pattern of GQDs/3D-G/PANI is similar to the combination of GQDs/3D-G and PANI diffraction pattern, indicating that GQDs/3D-G and PANI are physically mixed during solution mixing and doping and there is no change in crystal structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of thermal stability of materials
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 is the thermogravimetric curve of GQDs/3D-G/PANI doped materials with different GQDs/3D-G content. It is found that the weight loss of PANI between 100 °C–490 °C is relatively gentle, mainly due to the fracture of oxygen-containing functional groups on PANI. From 490 °C, the weight loss accelerated significantly, and reached about 65 %, which may be caused by the oxidative decomposition of PANI. The trend of thermal weightlessness and the initial temperature of weightlessness of doped composites are similar to that of PANI. That is, weightlessness is slow between 100 °C and 490 °C. Among them, when the content of GQDs/3D-G is 20 wt %, the composite powder loses about 25 %, but when the GQDs and PANI/3D-G weight ratio is 10 %, the composite powder weight loss of about 55 %, which is mainly due to 3D-G in a composite material with a mass ratio of 10 % is not sufficient to cause electrostatic interaction with excess PANI, and the thermal stability and synergistic effect of the material are not reflected. After the doping amount is increased, the binding effect between the two materials is enhanced, which can improve the overall thermal stability.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of different materials obtained during the preparation process. As shown in Fig. 3A, PANI is composed of numerous worm-like particles gathered together to form a stacking structure with different shapes. Fig. 3B shows the image of GQDs/3D-G. Although the self-assembled graphene quantum dots exist on the 3D-G surface, GQDs/3D-G still has an obvious three-dimensional network structure with rich folds, which indicates that 3D-G have extremely flexibility and the load of PANI provides a larger area. Fig. 3C is an image of GQDs/3D-G/PANI with GQDs/3D-G content of 10 wt%. Compared with pure PANI, large sized PANI particles were found in the composite powder with 10 wt% dopant, which are mainly due to the relatively weak electrostatic interaction and π-π interaction between GQDs/3D-G and PANI in the case of low GQDs/3D-G doping. It cannot effectively prevent the agglomeration of PANI and GQDs/3D-G themselves. Fig. 3D is an image of GQDs/3D-G/PANI with 30 wt % GQDs/3D-G content, PANI particles are attached to the surface of GQDs/3D-G. Under the common electrostatic and π-π interaction between them, the particle size of PANI decreased obviously, and the uniformity of doping increased. Thus, it can be judged that the increase of GQDs/3D-G content causes the increase of electrostatic and π-π interactions between GQDs/3D-G and PANI, which can effectively prevent the agglomeration of PANI and GQDs/3D-G.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of UV absorption of materials and analysis of influencing factors
ce:section.ce:para: In order to characterize the interface interaction between GQDs/ 3D-G and PANI, the UV–vis spectrum characterization of GQDs/3D-G/PANI mixed dispersion was performed, which was shown in Fig. 4 A. As can be seen in Fig. 4A, due to the reduction of oxygen-containing functional groups on 3D-G, GQDs/3D-G showed no characteristic peak in the range of 300 nm–800 nm. The absorption of PANI at 319 nm is caused by the transition of electrons within the benzene ring in its molecular structure from the HOMO orbital to the LOMO orbital, resulting in π-π* transition. The π-π* electronic transition in the quinone ring in the PANI molecular structure causes the UV absorption peak at 611 nm, but the intensity is significantly lower than the ultraviolet absorption peak at 319 nm. This is mainly because PANI is doped with a large amount of proton acid. The charge on the proton acid is transferred, resulting in the protonation of the imine ion on the quinone ring in the PANI molecular chain, and the charge balance is reached. This kind of charge balance model is to form the conjugate system. However, in the UV–vis spectrum of GQDs/3D-G/PANI, the UV–vis absorption peaks of PANI at 319 nm and 611 nm is red-shifted to 324 nm and 620 nm. This indicates that a strong π-π* interaction is occurred at the interface between PANI and GQDs/3D-G, resulting in enhanced electron delocalization of the conjugate ring on the PANI skeleton.

In order to further study the effect of the mass ratio of GQDs/3D-G to PANI on the intensity of the interfacial interaction, GQDs/3D-G dispersion solution (1.5 mg mL−1) of different volumes (0, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 75, 100, 150 and 200 μL) was added to PANI dispersion solution (5 mL, 1.5 mg mL−1) for UV–vis absorption characterization, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . With the addition of GQDs/3D-G solution, it can be seen from Fig. 4b that the characteristic absorption peak intensity of PANI located at 319 nm and 611 nm became weaker from top to bottom, accompanied by the gradual red shift of the peak position. This phenomenon further proves that there is a strong π-π interaction between PANI and GQDs/3D-G, and the electron delocalization of PANI skeleton is enhanced with the increase of GQDs/3D-G quantity. In addition, in order to detect the effect of the dispersion medium on the UV–vis absorption of the material, experiments were performed to add different volumes of hydrochloric acid solution (pH = 3) to the PANI dispersion, and the process was characterized by UV–vis. The results are shown in Fig. 4C. With the increase of the amount of hydrochloric acid, the absorption intensity of 319 nm and 611 nm is decreased slightly, but no red shift is occurred. This indicates that the concentration of PANI dispersion decreases slightly in the process of adding hydrochloric acid solution, so the intensity decreased, which confirmed that the dispersion medium had no effect on the UV–vis absorption of the material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of micromorphology of LBL films
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of LBL (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n membrane was analyzed by the SEM and the result obtained is shown in Fig. 5. Fig. 5a is the image of the first layer of PANI assembled, expressed as (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)0.5. It can be found that the PANI particles are scattered on the ITO surface, showing a small amount of PANI loading. Next, the first complete assembly of the film was completed by assembling and adsorbing the GQDs/3D-G layer, which is represented as (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)1, and its scanning electron microscope image is shown in Fig. 5B. The PANI particles were covered with GQDs/3D-G. At this time, it was observed that GQDs/3D-G and PANI was loosely covered the ITO surface. Since the amount of PANI and GQDs/3D-G loaded is very low, the surface coverage of ITO is also low. Fig. 5C is a SEM image of (GQDs /3D-G/PANI)5. It can be observed that with the increase of self-assembly layer, the LBL membrane became denser, basically covering the surface of ITO. Fig. 5D is the image as the number of layers of LBL reaches 10 layers. It is found that at this time (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10 was adhered uniformly to the ITO surface particles, and the network-like structure is obvious. Judging from the microscopic morphology, the formed abundant nanoscale pores are beneficial to ion adsorption and movement, which can support good electrochemical activity and catalytic performance. Afterwards, as the LBL process proceeded, the GQDs/3D-G and PANI began to gather significantly when the number of layers of the film further increased to 12 and 15 layers, which are shown in Fig. 6 e, 6f. Therefore, we selected (GQD/3D-G/PANI)10 for subsequent structural characterization and performance testing based on the information provided by the micromorphology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of surface elements and valence bonds of LBL films
ce:section.ce:para: The XPS analysis can further reveal the distribution of surface elements and valence bonds on LBL (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10 film. Fig. 6A shows the wide-range XPS spectrum of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10 thin film, indicating that C, N, and O elements are mainly present in the film. The CC characteristic absorption peaks and CC characteristic absorption peaks at 284.5 eV and 285.6 eV, respectively, can be seen from the C 1s spectra of the (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10 film in Fig.7 B, mainly due to the conjugate structure of GQDs, and the C elements in the LBL films mainly exist in the form of sp2 and sp3 hybrids. The characteristic peak of CO is appeared at 291.2 eV, and it was proved that the surface of GQDs contained -COOH, which provided the active sites for further composite modification of other conductive polymers with GQDs/3D-G. Besides, the N 1s peaks of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10 film were observed in Fig. 6C to split into four main peaks centered at 398.6 eV (-N=), 399.3 eV (-HN-), 401.5 eV (-HN+-) and 406.1 eV (-N+=). The characteristics of nitrogen cations and imine ions indicated a strong electrostatic interaction between GQDs/3D-G and PANI, which is consistent with the observed results from the previous UV–vis spectra. From the O 1s sub-peak spectrum in Fig. 6D, it can be observed that the characteristic peak of CO is at 531.2 eV, the characteristic peak of OCO is at 532.0 eV, and the characteristic peak of CO is at 535.9 eV, further confirmed the existence of GQDs on ITO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the internal structure of LBL films
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the internal structure of the (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n films, the unassembled GQDs/3D-G and (GQD/3D-G/PANI)10 were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, as shown in Fig. 7. Fig. 7A is a TEM image of GQDs/3D-G, showing the structural characteristics of translucent and spatially stacked wrinkles. Compared to a typical 3D-G transmission image, after compounding with GQDs, the shadow of the sheet increased and there are obvious signs of material attachment, which can be attributed to the self-assembly and recombination of graphene quantum dots on the three-dimensional graphene surface, forming a relatively uniform and regular composite layer. In Fig. 7B, after the occurrence of layer-by-layer self-assembly, GQDs/3D-G and PANI were superimposed on each other, and the original morphology of GQDs/3D-G was changed, confirming that the introduced PANI was uniformly assembled with it. At this time, both the characteristic spatial distribution of graphene can be clearly observed, and it can also be seen that the GQDs/3D-G and PANI are stacked evenly during the assembly process, showing a porous film covering on the film. The formation of this assembly structure, which can produce effective adsorption or intercalation between the surface or sandwich of PANI and GQDs/3D-G/PANI, and provide the strong support for the subsequent development of the properties of the (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the electrocatalytic performance of the LBL films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 showed the CV curves at the scan rate of 0.05 V s−1 in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH = 7.4) for different layers of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n films (n = 0, 0.5, 1,5, 10, 15) assembled on the ITO surface. It can be seen from Fig. 8A that the two CV curves of naked ITO and (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)0.5 did not show significant characteristic peaks, mainly due to the scarcity of electroactive materials on ITO. Whereas it can be seen (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)1 shows a pair of redox peaks at 0.05 V and -0.2 V in Fig. 8B, corresponding to the redox peaks of polyaniline. Moreover, as the self-assembly layer increased, the loading of the electroactive material on ITO increased, and the response current of the LBL thin films increased. However, when the number of self-assembly layers reached 15 layers, the redox current began to decrease significantly (Fig. 8B) [23], which is due to the film was so dense that it prevented the transfer of electrons from the electrolyte to the ITO surface.

Subsequently, 10−4 M H2O2 was added to the PBS solution, and a cyclic voltammetry test was performed. The (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n peak current (Fig. 9 C) was significantly increased compared to the peak current of Fig. 8B. The peak current at (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n/ITO where n = 1, 5, 10, 15 increased by 2.2 %, 23.75 %, 84.7 %, and 25.3 %, indicating that (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n has a significant catalytic decomposition of H2O2 on the surface of ITO, of which (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10/ITO has the highest response to H2O2. Meanwhile, the redox current of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)15/ITO (Fig. 8C) was significantly reduced, the reason kept consistent with the previous analysis that the dense surface limited the electron transfer.

Furthermore, it can be found that the oxidation potential of hydrogen peroxide (5 × 10–4 moL dm−3) on the surface of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10/ITO changed positively with the increase of scan rate (Fig. 9A) and accompanied by the gradual increase of peak current (Fig. 9B). Furthermore, from the curves A and B in Fig. 9B, it can be seen that the peak current of hydrogen peroxide was proportional to the square root of the scan rate during the increase of scanning speed from 10 to 150 mV s-1. It can be concluded that the oxidation of hydrogen peroxide on the electrode of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10/ITO should be a diffusion-controlled process.

In this paper, the method of self-assembly of GQDs on 3D-G surface to prepare GQDs/3D-G composite was used to solve the problem of easy agglomeration of graphene in the process of composite preparation. The composite was then mixed with conductive poly-aniline aqueous dispersion to form a GQDs/3D-G/PANI doped composite. The GQDs/3D-G/PANI film materials were prepared by layer-by-layer self-assembly through the strong electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged GQDs/3D-G and the positive-electric PANI and the driving of the π-π interaction. The LBL (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)n/ITO plate had obvious catalytic effect on trace H2O2, among which (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)10/ITO had the highest response to hydrogen peroxide. And the dense surface of (GQDs/3D-G/PANI)15/ITO will limit the electron transfer, which lead to a decrease in catalytic capacity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51262027), the State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing in NWPU (SKLSP201754), the Science and Technology Project Gansu Province (17YF1GA017), the Research Project of Higher Education in Gansu Province (2017A-002) and the Science and Technology Project Gansu Province (17JR5RA082).
